title: Stack, Queue & Deque
description: Unlock monotone stacks, BFS queues, and deque sliding-window tricks — the hidden engines behind dozens of Codeforces 1300–1600 problems.

sections.content: 🎯 Why These Structures Win Contests

Stack, queue, and deque are not just data structure textbook boxes — in competitive programming they encode **temporal order** and **monotone invariants**. A monotone stack turns next-greater-element from O(n²) to O(n). A BFS queue gives you shortest paths on unweighted graphs. A deque-based sliding window maximum beats the naive O(nk) with O(n). Learning when to use which is a force-multiplier for your rating.
📦 Stack — LIFO & the STL Interface

The STL `stack<T>` wraps a deque by default. Key operations: `push(x)`, `pop()`, `top()`, `empty()`, `size()` — all O(1). In contest code, a `vector<T>` used as a stack (`push_back`/`pop_back`/`back`) is equally common and slightly faster in practice.

sections.title: Balanced Parentheses — Classic Stack Application

sections.content: 🔥 Monotone Stack — The Contest Workhorse

A **monotone stack** maintains elements in either strictly increasing or decreasing order by popping elements that violate the invariant before each push. This gives O(n) amortized access to the *next greater*, *previous smaller*, *largest rectangle*, and similar problems that look O(n²) at first glance.

sections.label: Template: Next Greater Element
sections.content: Iterate left-to-right. Before pushing `arr[i]`, pop everything from the stack that is **<= arr[i]** (for next-greater). Those popped elements have their answer = `arr[i]`. Elements still in the stack at the end have no next-greater → answer -1. This works because each element is pushed and popped at most once: **O(n) total**.

sections.title: Next Greater Element for Every Index — O(n)
Largest Rectangle in Histogram — O(n) Monotone Stack

sections.label: 🚨 Contest Killer: Storing Values Instead of Indices in Monotone Stack
sections.content: Always store **indices** in the monotone stack, not values. You need the index to compute widths (for histogram), distances (for next-greater distance), and to update answer arrays at the correct position. Storing values loses positional information and makes the code incorrect or unextendable.
📬 Queue — FIFO & BFS

The STL `queue<T>` provides `push(x)`, `pop()`, `front()`, `back()`, `empty()` — all O(1). In graph problems, a queue is the backbone of **BFS** (Breadth-First Search), which finds shortest paths in **unweighted graphs**. A `deque<T>` can do everything a queue does, plus O(1) push/pop at both ends.

sections.title: BFS Shortest Path on an Unweighted Grid

sections.content: ⚡ Deque — Sliding Window Maximum (O(n))

The **sliding window maximum** problem: given array `arr` and window size `k`, find the maximum of every window `arr[i..i+k-1]`. The naive approach is O(nk). Using a **monotone deque** (decreasing order), each element is pushed and popped at most once, giving **O(n)**.

sections.title: Sliding Window Maximum — Monotone Deque O(n)

sections.label: Deque Front = Maximum, Back = Most Recent
sections.content: In the sliding window maximum deque: `dq.front()` is always the index of the **current maximum**. `dq.back()` is the most recently added index. Pop from the **front** when the window slides past that index. Pop from the **back** when a new element is >= the back's value (it can never be the max while the new element is in the window).
📐 Deque — 0-1 BFS (Replace Dijkstra for 0/1 Weights)

When edge weights are only 0 or 1, **0-1 BFS** using a deque beats Dijkstra's O((V+E) log V) with O(V+E). Push to the **front** for 0-weight edges (distance doesn't increase) and to the **back** for 1-weight edges. The deque stays nearly sorted by distance.

sections.title: 0-1 BFS — Shortest Path with 0 and 1 Edge Weights

sections.content: 📊 Complexity Reference Table

sections.label: Use `vector` as Stack for Speed
sections.content: In time-tight problems, replace `stack<int>` with `vector<int>` and use `push_back`/`pop_back`/`back`. It avoids the adapter overhead and gives you index access if needed. For the monotone stack, this is the standard contest idiom.
🧩 Common Monotone Stack Problem Patterns

items: **Next Greater Element**: iterate left-to-right, maintain increasing stack, pop when `arr[i] > stk.top()`
**Previous Smaller Element**: iterate left-to-right, maintain increasing stack, the top *before* push is the previous smaller
**Stock Span / Days Until Warmer**: same as NGE but store distances
**Largest Rectangle in Histogram**: increasing stack, pop and compute width when shorter bar arrives
**Maximal Rectangle in Binary Matrix**: per-row histogram + largest-rectangle
**Sum of Subarray Minimums**: combine previous-smaller and next-smaller stacks for contribution technique

sections.content: 🏋️ Interactive Exercises

sections.title: Exercise 1: Trace a Monotone Stack Build
sections.explanation: We build a non-increasing monotone stack (pop when new value > top). Process: i=0: push 0. Stack: [3]. i=1: a[1]=1 < a[0]=3, no pop. push 1. Stack: [3,1]. i=2: a[2]=4 > a[1]=1, pop 1; 4>3, pop 0. Push 2. Stack: [4]. i=3: a[3]=1 < a[2]=4, push 3. Stack: [4,1]. i=4: a[4]=5 > a[3]=1, pop 3; 5>4, pop 2. Push 4. Stack: [5]. Pop all in LIFO order: values at index 4=5, then nothing else — wait, let me retrace. After i=3: stack indices=[2,3], values=[4,1]. After i=4: pop index 3 (val 1 < 5), pop index 2 (val 4 < 5), push 4. Stack=[4] (index). Print popping: a[4]=5. Only one element. Output: '5 '. Wait — rechecking i=3: a[3]=1, stk top is index 2 (val 4), 1 < 4 so NO pop, push 3. Stack indices=[2,3]. i=4: a[4]=5. top=index 3 (val 1), 1 < 5 → pop. top=index 2 (val 4), 4 < 5 → pop. stack empty, push 4. Final stack: [4]. Pop: a[4]=5. Hmm only one. Let me re-examine — condition is `a[stk.top()] < a[i]`, so it's strictly less than. At i=2: top=1(val 1), 1<4 pop; top=0(val 3), 3<4 pop; push 2. At i=4: top=3(val 1), 1<5 pop; top=2(val 4), 4<5 pop; push 4. Stack at end: [4]. Pop gives: 5. But wait the expected output says '5 4 1'. Let me re-examine more carefully... At i=3, a[3]=1. Top of stack is index 2 (val 4). 4 < 1? No. So we just push 3. Stack = [2, 3]. At i=4, a[4]=5. Top=3(val 1), 1<5 yes → pop. Top=2(val 4), 4<5 yes → pop. Stack empty, push 4. Final stack = [4]. Only one element: value 5. The answer should be: 5.

sections.title: Exercise 2: Sliding Window Deque Trace
sections.explanation: i=0: dq=[0](val 4). i<2, no output. i=1: a[1]=2<=a[0]=4? No, 2<=4 yes → pop 0. Push 1. dq=[1](val 2). i<2, no output. Wait: condition is a[dq.back()] <= a[i]. dq.back()=0, a[0]=4. Is 4<=2? No. So don't pop, just push 1. dq=[0,1](vals 4,2). i=2: a[2]=7. dq.back()=1, a[1]=2<=7 yes → pop. dq.back()=0, a[0]=4<=7 yes → pop. dq empty, push 2. dq=[2](val 7). i>=2: output a[2]=7. i=3: front=2, 2<=3-3=0? No. a[dq.back()]=a[2]=7<=a[3]=3? No. push 3. dq=[2,3]. i>=2: output a[dq.front()]=a[2]=7. i=4: front=2, 2<=4-3=1? No. a[dq.back()]=a[3]=3<=a[4]=5? Yes → pop 3. dq.back()=2, a[2]=7<=5? No. push 4. dq=[2,4]. i>=2: output a[2]=7. Final output: 7 7 7.

sections.title: Exercise 3: Daily Temperatures (Next Warmer Day)
sections.approach: Classic monotone stack problem. Iterate left-to-right. Maintain a stack of indices whose 'days until warmer' is not yet answered. When we reach day i with temp[i], pop all stack indices j where temp[j] < temp[i]. The answer for those j is i - j. Push i onto the stack. Remaining elements at the end have answer 0.
sections.explanation: Each index is pushed once and popped at most once → O(n) total. The moment we find a warmer day i for a stored day j, we compute i-j. For temps=[73,74,75,71,69,72,76,73]: Process i=0: push 0. i=1: temp[0]=73<74, pop, ans[0]=1, push 1. i=2: temp[1]=74<75, pop, ans[1]=1, push 2. i=3: 75>71, push 3. i=4: 71>69, push 4. i=5: 69<72, pop ans[4]=1; 71<72, pop ans[3]=2; 75>72, push 5. i=6: 72<76 pop ans[5]=1; 75<76 pop ans[2]=4; 74<76 pop ans[1] already popped; stack has index 1? No. Let me retrace: after i=2, stack=[2]. After i=3: push 3, stack=[2,3]. After i=4: push 4, stack=[2,3,4]. i=5: 69<72 pop 4 ans[4]=1; 71<72 pop 3 ans[3]=2; 75>72 stop. push 5. stack=[2,5]. i=6: 72<76 pop 5 ans[5]=1; 75<76 pop 2 ans[2]=4; push 6. stack=[6]. i=7: 76>73 push 7. stack=[6,7]. End: ans[6]=ans[7]=0. Final: [1,1,4,2,1,1,0,0]. ✓

sections.title: Exercise 4: Largest Rectangle in Histogram
sections.approach: Use an increasing monotone stack of indices. For each bar i, if h[i] < h[stk.top()], we know the top bar's maximum extent to the right ends at i. Pop it and compute width as i - stk.top() - 1 (or i if stack is empty). After processing all bars, flush the stack with a sentinel bar of height 0 at position n.
sections.explanation: When we pop index `top`, its height h[top] is the rectangle's height. The rectangle extends left as far as the new stack top (exclusive) and right as far as i (exclusive), giving width = i - stk.top() - 1. If the stack is empty, the rectangle spans from 0 to i-1, so width = i. For h=[2,1,5,6,2,3]: The largest rectangle comes from h[2]=5 and h[3]=6 spanning width 2, giving area 10. Time: O(n) — each bar pushed/popped once. Space: O(n) for stack.

sections.title: Exercise 5: Minimum of Each Sliding Window (Deque)
sections.approach: Same as sliding window maximum but maintain a monotone INCREASING deque (pop back when new element is <= back's value, since larger elements can never be a future minimum while the smaller one is in the window). Front of deque is always the index of the current window minimum.
sections.explanation: The only change from sliding-window-maximum is the pop-back condition: for minimum we keep the deque increasing (pop when arr[dq.back()] >= arr[i]). Front always holds the smallest element in the current window. For arr=[3,-1,-3,5,3,6,7], k=3: Windows are [3,-1,-3]→-3, [-1,-3,5]→-3, [-3,5,3]→-3, [5,3,6]→3, [3,6,7]→3. Output: -1 -3 -3 3 3. Wait: window 0 is [3,-1,-3] with min -3, not -1. Let me recheck: indices 0,1,2 = values 3,-1,-3, min=-3. But expected says -1 first? Let me recheck the example: the expected output for max problem is different. For min: windows [3,-1,-3]=-3, [-1,-3,5]=-3, [-3,5,3]=-3, [5,3,6]=3, [3,6,7]=3. Output: -3 -3 -3 3 3. Time: O(n). Space: O(k).

sections.content: 📋 Quick Reference Summary
